text: But he gave none of the wagons or oxen to the Kohathite division, since they were required to carry the sacred objects of the Tabernacle on their shoulders.

content:
We have now seen that the position of the altar before the veil speaks of Christ’s intercession at the very presence of God, that the continuous morning and evening burning speaks of His unceasing intercession, and that the unique sacred incense speaks of Christ as the irreplaceable, sweet-smelling offering before the Father. Today we study three more features: the fire that lit the incense, the restriction against strange incense, and the crown that ran around the altar’s rim.

The fire that burned on the golden altar did not come from the priests’ own source. It was taken from the bronze altar of burnt offering that stood in the outer court the altar where the animal sacrifices were made, where the blood of atonement was shed. The coal was carried from that altar into the Holy Place and placed on the golden altar to light the incense. This connection was not incidental. The fragrance of the incense depended entirely on the fire of the sacrifice. Without the sacrifice, there was no fire. Without the fire, there was no incense. Without the incense, there was no fragrance before God. The whole chain began at the altar of blood.

This speaks of a truth that runs through the entire New Testament: our access to God, our prayer, our worship, our approach to the Father; all of it is possible only because of the sacrifice of Christ. Hebrews 10:19-22 says: “Therefore, brothers, since we have confidence to enter the holy places by the blood of Jesus, by the new and living way that He opened for us through the curtain, that is, through His flesh, and since we have a great priest over the house of God,
let us draw near with a true heart in full assurance of faith.” The way into the presence is through the flesh of Christ through the sacrifice. The incense of our prayer is lit by the fire of His atonement. Remove the death of Christ and the fire goes out. Remove the fire and the incense cannot burn. Remove the sacrifice and the prayer has no way to rise.

Leviticus 10:1-2 records what happened when Nadab and Abihu brought fire that did not come from the prescribed source. They offered strange fire before Yahweh and were consumed. The lesson was absolute: the fire must come from the right place. There is no alternative route to God’s presence. There is no self-generated prayer that rises acceptably before Yahweh apart from the sacrifice of Christ. Every religion, every spiritual practice, every meditation method that seeks to approach God without the ransom sacrifice is strange fire. And the warning of the tabernacle is clear.

Exodus 30:9 gives one of the most absolute restrictions in all the instructions for the tabernacle: “You shall not offer unauthorized incense on it, or a burnt offering, or a grain offering, and you shall not pour a drink offering on it.”
And verses 37-38 add that no one was to reproduce the incense formula for any other purpose. The formula was sacred, exclusive, and belonging entirely to Yahweh. Any attempt to create a substitute was punishable by being cut off from the people. The incense that rose before God was one specific formula and nothing else could take its place.

In the same way, the mediator who presents our worship and intercession before the Father is one specific person and nothing else can take His place.
1 Timothy 2:5 says: “For there is one God, and there is one mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus, who gave Himself as a ransom for all.” One mediator. Not many. Not one among several options. But ONE.
The incense formula was unique and irreplaceable. So is the mediation of Christ. Any approach to God that bypasses Christ; any prayer addressed to a human mediator, any spiritual practice that claims to reach God by another route, any religion that adds to or replaces the mediation of Christ is unauthorized incense. It has no prescribed fire. It has no formula that belongs to Yahweh. And it does not reach the throne.

This is not a restrictive truth. It is a liberating one. Because there is one mediator and He always lives to intercede, every person who comes to God through Christ has the full, undivided, unceasing attention of that mediator. We do not share a mediator who is distracted or overwhelmed. Hebrews 4:16 says:
“Let us then with confidence draw near to the throne of grace, that we may receive mercy and find grace to help in time of need.” Confidence. Because the formula is not ours to produce. It is God’s; hence he sent his son to redeem us.

Running around the top edge of the golden altar was a gold crown a gold rim or border called a crown, exactly as the Table of Showbread had a crown. This crown served a practical purpose, keeping the coals and the incense from falling off the altar. But as we have already seen in studying the table, the crown declares the royalty of what it rests on. The altar of incense wore a crown. It was not merely priestly furniture. It was the ministry of a King.

Hebrews 2:9 says: “We see Him who for a little while was made lower than the angels, namely Jesus, now crowned with glory and honour because of the suffering of death.” The crown came after the suffering. The incense altar was used by the priest to offer what the sacrifice on the bronze altar had made possible. The bronze altar came first. The incense altar followed. In the same way, his ransom came first. The exaltation followed. The crown on the incense altar is the crown of the risen, ascended, glorified Christ; now at the right hand of the Father, crowned with glory and honour, presenting the incense of His perfect intercession before the throne.

And Revelation 19:12 says He wears many crowns. The single crown on the incense altar is only a glimpse. The one who serves as our intercessor at the throne of the Father is not a defeated priest barely gaining an audience. He is the crowned King of kings, whose intercession is heard because of who He is and what He has done.
When He intercedes, the Father listens. When He presents our prayers in His own fragrant mediation, the Father receives them. Hebrews 7:26 describes this High Priest as “holy, innocent, unstained, separated from sinners, and exalted above the heavens” not standing before the veil as the earthly altar stood, but exalted above the heavens, crowned and reigning, with the incense of His perfected intercession rising before the Father without ceasing.

title: Prayer
text: Blessed are You, Yahweh our God, who hears the voice of prayer and supplication (1 Kings 8:30). We thank You that the fire of Your Son’s sacrifice lit the altar of prayer for us. We thank You that because of the cross there is now a living and burning way into Your presence; that the incense did not go cold when He died but blazed brighter than ever when He rose and ascended to Your right hand. Forgive us for the times we have tried to approach You with strange fire; our own merit, our own religious effort, our own goodness rather than through the one mediator You have given us. Teach us to come to You only through Christ, trusting His fragrance and not our own. And help us to see Him as He truly is; not merely as the priest but as the crowned King at Your right hand, whose intercession is heard because He reigns. May we draw near with confidence. In Christ’s name, amen.
